fact_pattern: Ramesh agreed to supply 500 kg of wheat to Suresh by March 1st for Rs. 50,000. Ramesh failed to deliver. Suresh had to buy from another supplier at Rs. 65,000.
applicable_statutes: Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 73 - Compensation for loss

precedents.rationale: Damages naturally arising from breach are recoverable.
Seller liable for market price difference upon breach.

gold_label_reasoning: Clear breach of contract. Damages of Rs.15,000 recoverable under Section 73.

fact_pattern: Priya hired a contractor to renovate her shop within 30 days for Rs. 2,00,000. The contractor completed the work in 45 days. Priya refused to pay claiming full breach.
applicable_statutes: Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 55 - Time as essence of contract

precedents.rationale: Substantial performance may still entitle contractor to payment minus damages.

gold_label_reasoning: Contractor liable for delay damages but entitled to payment for completed work.

fact_pattern: A hospital left a surgical sponge inside a patient during an operation. The patient suffered infection 3 months later. The hospital claims the infection was unrelated.

precedents.case_name: Jacob Mathew v State of Punjab (2005)
precedents.rationale: Res ipsa loquitur applies in medical negligence cases.

precedents.case_name: Spring Meadows Hospital v Harjol Ahluwalia (1998)
precedents.rationale: Hospital vicariously liable for staff negligence.

gold_label_reasoning: Res ipsa loquitur — sponge inside patient speaks for itself. Hospital liable.

fact_pattern: A pedestrian jaywalked and was hit by a car travelling 10 km/h over the speed limit. Both parties claim the other is at fault.

precedents.rationale: Contributory negligence reduces but does not eliminate liability.

gold_label_reasoning: Contributory negligence of pedestrian is primary cause. Driver liability reduced significantly.

fact_pattern: Grandfather died without a will. Three grandchildren claim inheritance. Child A has a registered sale deed from grandfather dated 2018. Child B claims the deed was signed under coercion. Child C claims adverse possession for 15 years.

precedents.case_name: Ravinder Kaur Grewal v Manjit Kaur (2019)
precedents.rationale: 12 years continuous possession can override registered title.
Registered sale deed presumed valid unless fraud proven by clear evidence.

gold_label_reasoning: Registered sale deed (Child A) presumed valid. Coercion unsubstantiated. Adverse possession claim insufficient without continuous possession proof.
